content:
Consider a frequency-modulated (FM) signal

$$ f(t)=A_c \cos \left(2 \pi f_c t+3 \sin \left(2 \pi f_1 t\right)+4 \sin \left(6 \pi f_1 t\right)\right) $$

where $A_c$ and $f_c$ are, respectively, the amplitude and frequency (in Hz ) of the carrier waveform. The frequency $f_1$ is in Hz , and assume that $f_c>100 f_1$.

The peak frequency deviation of the FM signal in Hz is $\qquad$
explanation:
$$ f(t)=A_c \cos \left[2 \pi f_c t+3 \sin \left(2 \pi f_1 t\right)+4 \sin \left(6 \pi f_1 t\right)\right] $$

General expression of angle modulated signal,

$$ s(t)=A_c \cos \left[2 \pi f_c t+\phi(t)\right] $$

From comparison: $\phi(t)=3 \sin 2 \pi f_1 t+4 \sin 6 \pi f_1 t$

Peak frequency deviation:

$$ \begin{aligned} \Delta f=\left|\frac{1}{2 \pi} \frac{d}{d t} \phi(t)\right|_{\max } & =\left|\frac{1}{2 \pi}\left[3 \times 2 \pi f_1 \cos 2 \pi f_1 t+4 \times 6 \pi f_1 \cos 6 \pi f_1 t\right]\right|_{\max } \\ & =\left|3 f_1 \cos 2 \pi f_1 t+12 f_1 \cos 6 \pi f_1 t\right|_{\max }=3 f_1+12 f_1=15 f_1 \end{aligned} $$

content:
Consider a message signal $m(t)$ which is bandlimited to $[-W, W]$, where $W$ is in Hz . Consider the following two modulation schemes for the message signal:

Double sideband-suppressed carrier (DSB-SC):

$$ f_{\mathrm{DSB}}(t)=A_c m(t) \cos \left(2 \pi f_c t\right) $$

Amplitude modulation (AM):

$$ f_{\mathrm{AM}}(t)=A_c(1+\mu m(t)) \cos \left(2 \pi f_c t\right) $$

Here, $A_c$ and $f_c$ are the amplitude and frequency (in Hz ) of the carrier, respectively. In the case of AM, $\mu$ denotes the modulation index.

Consider the following statements:

(i) An envelope detector can be used for demodulation in the DSB-SC scheme if $m(t)>0$ for all $t$.

(ii) An envelope detector can be used for demodulation in the AM scheme only if $m(t)>0$ for all $t$.

Which of the following options is/are correct?
explanation: If $m(t)>0$ for all $t$, envelope detector can be used for demodulation of DSB-SC signal. Even for $m(t)<0$, envelope detector can be used for demodulation of AM signal.

content:
An amplitude modulator has output (in Volts)

$$s(t) = A \cos(400 \pi t) + B \cos(360 \pi t) + B \cos(440 \pi t)$$.

The carrier power normalized to $1\Omega$ resistance is 50 Watts. The ratio of the total sideband power to the total power is 1/9. The value of $B$ (in Volts, rounded off to two decimal places) is _______.
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Given : }\\ &\begin{aligned} s(t)=A \cos (400 \pi t)+B & \cos (360 \pi t) +B \cos (440 \pi t) \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

Carrier power $\left(P_c\right)=50$ watts

Ratio of total side band power to total power.

$$ \frac{P_{S B}}{P_t}=\frac{1}{9} $$

For a single tone sinusoidal signal, expression of AM signal is given by,

$$ \begin{aligned} & s(t)=A_c\left[1+m_a \cos \omega_m t\right] \cos \omega_c t \\ & s(t)=A_c \cos \omega_c t+\frac{A_c m_a}{2} \cos \left(\omega_c+\omega_m\right) t \\ & \quad \quad+\frac{A_c m_a}{2} \cos \left(\omega_c-\omega_m\right) t \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\ldots(\mathrm{ii}) \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Comparing equation (i) and (ii) }\\ &A_c=A, \frac{A_c m_a}{2}=B \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(iii)\end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Power in carrier signal is given by }\\ &\begin{aligned} & P_c=\frac{A_c^2}{2}=\frac{A^2}{2} \\ & \frac{A^2}{2}=50 \\ & A=10 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

For sinusoidal signal ratio of side band power to total power is given by

$$ \begin{aligned} \frac{P_{S B}}{P_t} & =\frac{\frac{P_c m_a^2}{2}}{P_c\left[1+\frac{m_a^2}{2}\right]}=\frac{\frac{m_a^2}{2}}{\frac{2+m_a^2}{2}} \\ \frac{P_{S B}}{P_t} & =\frac{m_a^2}{2+m_a^2} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Hence, } \frac{P_{S B}}{P_t}=\frac{1}{9} \quad \text { (Given) }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{9}=\frac{m_a^2}{2+m_a^2} \\ & 2+m_a^2=9 m_a^2 \\ & 2=8 m_a^2 \\ & m_a^2=\frac{1}{4} \\ & m_a=\frac{1}{2} \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

From equation (iii)

$$ \begin{aligned} & \frac{A_c m_a}{2}=B \\ & \frac{(10)(1 / 2)}{2}=B \\ & \frac{10}{4}=B \\ & B=2.5 \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct answer is $\mathbf{2 . 5}$.

content: Consider a super heterodyne receiver tuned to 600 kHz . If the local oscillator feeds a 1000 kHz signal to the mixer. The image frequency (in integer) is $\_\_\_\_$ kHz .
explanation:
$$ \text { From question, we can say that } $$

$$ \begin{aligned} & & f_{I F} & =f_{L O}-f_s \\ & \text { or, } & & =1000 \mathrm{kHz}-600 \mathrm{kHz}=400 \mathrm{kHz} \\ \therefore & & f_{s i} & =f_s+2 f_{\mathrm{IF}} \\ & & & =600 \mathrm{kHz}+2 \times 400 \mathrm{kHz} \\ \text { And, } & & f_{s i} & =1400 \mathrm{kHz} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \text { Hence, the correct answer is }(1400 \text { to } 1400) \text {. } $$

content: Consider a carrier signal which is amplitude modulated by a single - tone sinusoidal message signal with a modulation index of $50 \%$. If the carrier and one of the side bands are suppressed in the modulated signal, the percentage of power saved (rounded off to one decimal place) is $\_\_\_\_$
explanation:
Solution: For SSB-SC, As we know that\% power saved

$$ =\frac{P_C+P_{S B}}{P t}=\frac{P_C\left[\frac{\mu^2}{4}+1\right]}{P_C\left[1+\frac{\mu^2}{2}\right]} \text { for } S S B-S C $$

or,  $$ =\frac{1+\frac{\mu^2}{4}}{1+\frac{\mu^2}{2}} $$

or, we get $\%$ power saved $=\frac{\mu^2+4}{4+2 \mu^2}$

As mentioned we have $\mu=0.5$

Therefore % power saving $=\frac{4+0.25}{4+2 \times 0.25} \times 100=94.44$.

Hence, the correct answer is (94.4 to 94.5).

content:

f<sub>1F</sub> $$\leq$$ f<sub>L0</sub>.It is
required that the image frequencies fall outside the 58 MHz – 68 MHz band. The minimum
required
f<sub>1F</sub> (in MHz) is ___________.

denotes the Hilbert
transform of m(t) and the bandwidth of m(t) is very small compared to f<sub>c</sub>. The signal s(t) is a

t+2sin(8000$$\mathrm\pi$$t) +

content: $$v\left(t\right)\;=5\left[\cos\left(10^6\mathrm{πt}\right)\;-\;\sin\left(10^3\mathrm{πt}\right)\times\sin\left(10^6\mathrm{πt}\right)\right]$$ represents

Let a frequency modulated (FM) signal $$x(t) = A\cos ({\omega _c}t + {k_f}\int_{ - \infty }^t {m(\lambda )d\lambda )} $$, where $$m(t)$$ is a message signal of bandwidth W. It is passed through a non-linear system with output $$y(t) = 2x(t) + 5{(x(t))^2}$$. Let $${B^T}$$ denote the FM bandwidth. The minimum value of $${\omega _c}$$ required to recover $$x(t)$$ from $$y(t)$$ is
explanation:
Given : $x(t)=A \cos \left(\omega_c t+k_f \int\limits_{-\infty}^t m(\lambda) d \lambda\right)$

Also, $y(t)=2 x(t)+5 x^2(t)$

$\begin{aligned} & y(t)=2 A \cos \left(\omega_c t+k_f \int\limits_{-\infty}^t m(\lambda) d \lambda\right)+5 A^2 \cos ^2\left(\omega_c t+k_f \int\limits_{-\infty}^t m(\lambda) d \lambda\right) \\\\ & y(t)=2 A \cos \left(\omega_c t+k_f \int\limits_{-\infty}^t m(\lambda) d \lambda\right)+5 A^2\left(\frac{1+\cos \left(2 \omega_c t+2 k_f \int\limits_{-\infty}^t m(\lambda) d \lambda\right)}{2}\right)\end{aligned}$

$$ y(t)=2 A \cos \left(\omega_c t+k_f \int\limits_{-\infty}^t m(\lambda) d \lambda\right)+\frac{5 A^2}{2} \cos \left(2 \omega_c t+k_f \int\limits_{-\infty}^t m(\lambda) d \lambda\right)+\frac{5 A^2}{2} $$

Om plotting the frequency spectrum of $y(t)$

To recover $x(t)$ from $y(t)$

$$ \begin{aligned} & 2 \omega_c-B_T>\omega_c+\frac{B_T}{2} \\\\ & \omega_c>\frac{3 B_T}{2} \end{aligned} $$

$\therefore$ Minimum value of $\omega_c$ will be $\left(\omega_c\right)_{\min }=\frac{3 B_T}{2}$

Hence, the correct option is (B).

content: Let x$$_1$$(t) and x$$_2$$(t) be two band-limited signals having bandwidth $$B=4\pi\times10^3$$ rad/s each. In the figure below, the Nyquist sampling frequency, in rad/s, required to sample y(t), is
explanation:
Given that $x_1(t)$ and $x_2(t)$ are bandlimited to $4 \pi \times 10^3 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{sec}$. $x_1(t)$ and $x_2(t)$ can be represented in frequency domain as shown below,

From the given figure,

$$ y(t)=x_1(t) \cos \left(12 \pi \times 10^3 t\right)+x_2(t) \cos \left(4 \pi \times 10^3 t\right) $$

The spectrum of $y(t)$ can be drawn as shown below

$\therefore$ Maximum frequency of $Y(\omega)$ is $\omega_m=16 \pi \times 10^3 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{sec}$

Nyquist sampling rate will be $\omega_s=2 \omega_m=32 \pi \times 10^3 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{sec}$

Hence, the correct option is (D).

content:
Consider an FM broadcast that employs the pre-emphasis filter with frequency response

$${H_{pe}}(\omega ) = 1 + {{j\omega } \over {{\omega _0}}}$$,

where $$\omega$$0 = 104 rad/sec.

For the network shown in the figure to act as a corresponding de-emphasis filter, the appropriate pairs of (R, C) values is/are ____________.
explanation:
Given frequency response of pre-emphasis filter,

$${H_{pe}}(\omega ) = 1 + {{j\omega } \over {{\omega _0}}}$$ where, $${\omega _0} = {10^4}{{rad} \over {\sec }}$$

Deemphasis filter given as,

$${H_{De}}(\omega ) = {1 \over {1 + j\omega RC}}\buildrel {} \over \longrightarrow \left| {{H_{De}}(\omega )} \right| = {1 \over {\sqrt {1 + {{(\omega RC)}^2}} }}$$

Relationship between pre-emphasis and De-emphasis systems is

$$\left| {{H_{Pe}}(\omega )} \right| = {1 \over {\left| {{H_{De}}(\omega )} \right|}}$$

$${\left| {{H_{Pe}}(\omega )} \right|^2} = {1 \over {{{\left| {{H_{De}}(\omega )} \right|}^2}}}$$

$$1 + {\left( {{\omega \over {{\omega _0}}}} \right)^2} = 1 + {(\omega RC)^2}$$

$${1 \over {{\omega _0}}} = RC \Rightarrow RC = {10^{ - 4}}$$

Option (a) only satisfies the required condition.

content: A sinusoidal message signal having root mean square value of 4 V and frequency of 1 kHz is fed to a phase modulator with phase deviation constant $2 \mathrm{rad} /$ volt. If the carrier signal is $c(t)=2 \cos \left(2 \pi 10^6 t\right)$, the maximum instantaneous frequency of the phase modulated signal (rounded off to one decimal place) is $\_\_\_\_$ Hz.
explanation:
From the question, we have

$$ \begin{aligned} & V_{\mathrm{rms}}=\frac{A_m}{\sqrt{2}}=4 \\ & \begin{aligned} A_m & =4 \times \sqrt{2} \text { volts } \\ f_m & =1 \mathrm{KHz} \\ \Delta \varphi & =2 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{volt}=K_p m(t) \\ f_c & =10^6 \\ A c & =2 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

Now on using the below formula, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} f_i & =A c \cos [\theta(t)] \\ & =A c \cos \left[2 \pi f_c t+K_p m(t)\right] \\ or,f_i & =\frac{1}{2 \pi} \frac{d}{d t}[\theta(f)] \end{aligned} $$

Also we have $\left(f_i\right)_{\text {max }}=f_c+\frac{k p}{2 \pi}\left[\frac{d m(t)}{d t}\right]_{\text {max }}$

or, $\left[\frac{d m(t)}{d t}\right]_{\max }=2 \pi \times 10^3 \times 4 \sqrt{2}$

$$ \therefore \quad\left(f_i\right)_{\max }=1000 \times 10^3+\frac{2}{2 \pi}\left[2 \pi \times 4 \sqrt{2} \times 10^3\right] $$

$$ \begin{aligned} or,& =10^6+8 \sqrt{2} \times 10^3 \\ or,& =(1000+11.3137) \mathrm{KHz} \\ & =1011313.7 \mathrm{~Hz} \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the correct answer is (1011313 to 1011314).

content:
$S_{P M}(t)$ and $S_{F M}(t)$ are defined below, are the phase modulated and the frequency modulated waveforms, respectively, corresponding to the message signal $m(t)$ shown in the figure.

$$ \begin{aligned} & S_{P M}(t)=\cos \left[1000 \pi t+k_p m(t)\right] \\ & S_{F M}(t)=\cos \left[1000 \pi t+k_f \int_{-\infty}^t m(\tau) d \tau\right] \end{aligned} $$

Where $k_p$ is the phase deviation constant in radians/volt and $k_f$ is the frequency deviation constant in radians/second/volt. If the highest instantaneous frequencies of $S_{P M}(t)$ and $S_{F M}(t)$ are same, then the value of the ratio $\frac{k_p}{k_f}$ is $\_\_\_\_$ seconds.
explanation:
Given : Phase modulated waveform,

$$ S_{P M}(t)=\cos \left[1000 \pi t+k_p m(t)\right] $$

Frequency modulated waveform,

$$ S_{F M}(t)=\cos \left[1000 \pi t+k_f \int_{-\infty}^t m(\tau) d \tau\right] $$

$S_{P M}$ is phase modulated signal with instantaneous phase

$$ \theta_i(t)=1000 \pi t+k_p m(t) $$

and  $$ k_p=\text { phase deviation constant }(\mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{volt}) $$

$S_{F M}(t)=$ frequency modulated signal with instantaneous phase

$$ \theta_i(t)=1000 \pi t+k_f \int_{-\infty}^t m(\tau) d \tau $$

Where $k_f=$ frequency deviation constant

Given that, highest instantaneous frequency are same

$$ \left.\left(f_i\right)\right|_{P M_{\max }}=\left.\left(f_i\right)\right|_{F M_{\max }} $$

From the relation,

$$ f_i(t)=\frac{1}{2 \pi} \frac{d \theta_i(t)}{d t} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Instantaneous angular frequency of PM waveform, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & \left.\left(f_i\right)\right|_{P M}=\frac{1}{2 \pi} \frac{d}{d t}\left[1000 \pi t+k_p m(t)\right] \\ & \left.\left(f_i\right)\right|_{P M}=\frac{1}{2 \pi}[1000 \pi]+\left.\frac{k_p}{2 \pi} \frac{d m(t)}{d t}\right|_{\max } \\ & \left.\left(f_i\right)\right|_{P M}=500+\left.\frac{k_p}{2 \pi} \frac{d m(t)}{d t}\right|_{\max } \\ & \left.\frac{d m(t)}{d t}\right|_{\max }=5 \\ & \left.f_i(t)\right|_{P M}=500+\frac{k_p}{2 \pi} \times 5 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Instantaneous angular frequency of FM waveform, }\\ &\begin{aligned} \left.f_i(t)\right|_{F M} & =\frac{1}{2 \pi} \frac{d}{d t}\left[1000 \pi t+k_f \int_{-\infty}^t m(\tau) d \tau\right] \\ & =\frac{1}{2 \pi}[1000 \pi]+\left.\frac{k_f}{2 \pi} m(t)\right|_{\max } \\ \left.m(t)\right|_{\max } & =10 \\ \left.f_i(t)\right|_{F M} & =500+\frac{k_f}{2 \pi} \times 10 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Equating equations (i) and (ii), }\\ &\begin{aligned} & 500+\frac{k_p}{2 \pi} \times 5=500+\frac{k_f}{2 \pi} \times 10 \\ & \frac{k_p}{k_f}=\frac{10}{5}=2 \end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \text { Hence, the value of the ratio } \frac{k_p}{k_f} \text { is } 2 \text {. } $$

content: For the modulated signal $x(t)=m(t) \cos \left(2 \pi f_c t\right)$, the message signal $m(t)=4 \cos (1000 \pi t)$ and the carrier frequency $f_c$ is 1 MHz . The signal $x(t)$ is passed through a demodulator, as shown in figure below. The output $y(t)$ of the demodulator is
explanation:
$$ \text { Given demodulator circuit is shown below, } $$

$$ \text { Ideal LPF has cut off frequency } 510 \mathrm{~Hz} $$

Given $x(t)=m(t) \cos \left(2 \pi f_c t\right)$ where

$$ m(t)=4 \cos (1000 \pi t) \text { and } f_c=1 \mathrm{MHz} $$

From the figure,

$$ \begin{aligned} & p(t)=x(t) \cos \left(2 \pi\left(f_c+40\right) t\right) \\ & p(t)=m(t) \cos \left(2 \pi f_c t\right) \cos \left(2 \pi f_c t+80 \pi t\right) \\ & p(t)=\frac{m(t)}{2}\left[\cos \left(4 \pi f_c t+80 \pi t\right)+\cos (80 \pi t)\right] \\ & p(t)=2 \cos (1000 \pi t)\left[\cos \left(4 \pi f_c t+80 \pi t\right)+\cos (80 \pi t)\right] \\ & p(t)=\underbrace{\cos \left(4 \pi f_c t+1080 \pi t\right)}_{x_1}+\underbrace{\cos \left(4 \pi f_c t-920 \pi t\right)}_{x_2}+\underbrace{\cos (1080 \pi t)}_{f=540 \mathrm{~Hz}}+\underbrace{\cos (920 \pi t)}_{f=460 \mathrm{~Hz}} \end{aligned} $$

As LPF has cut-off frequency of 510 Hz or $1020 \pi \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$, so components $x_1, x_2$ and $x_3$ will be attenuated by LPF.

$$ y(t)=\cos (920 \pi t) $$

Hence, the correct option is (C).

content:

sinusoidal modulating signal. The maximum percentage of modulation is 50%. If it is reduced

c(t) and m(t) are used to generate an AM signal. The modulation index of the generated AM signal is 0.5. Then the quantity $$\frac{Total\;sideband\;power}{Carrier\;power}$$ is

waveform)<sup>3</sup>, then by using Carson’s approximation, the bandwidth of y(t) around

Which of the following demodulator(s) can be used for demodulating the signal

A superheterodyne radio receiver with an intermediate frequency of 455 KHz is
tuned to a station operating at 1200 KHz. The associated image frequency is

The maximum power efficiency of an AM modulator is
